primary_diagnosis.name: Cutaneous mast cell activation flare (isolated urticaria/flushing) — no anaphylaxis

pathophysiology_summary: Mast cell degranulation releases histamine and other mediators into the skin, producing rapid widespread urticaria, flushing and dermatographism, often triggered by heat or friction. The danger is progression to systemic anaphylaxis (airway oedema, bronchospasm, hypotension, GI symptoms). In this case the reaction is confined to the skin: the patient denies breathing difficulty, throat tightness, voice change, dizziness and GI symptoms, and in person the airway is patent, chest clear and BP normal. Telehealth cannot examine the airway/chest or measure BP, but the anaphylaxis screen is history-based, so the correct approach is a careful systemic screen, antihistamine up-dosing and cooling, an adrenaline-autoinjector/action-plan check (given MCAS and living alone), and a very low threshold to call 000 if any systemic feature develops — not an in-person injection or a reflexive ambulance for isolated urticaria.

differential.evidence_basis: Explosive urticaria/flushing can be the first sign of anaphylaxis; screen airway/breathing/circulation/GI immediately.
Known MCAS with heat-triggered urticaria/dermatographism is most likely.
Less likely given the rapidity, intense itch and dermatographism.
No airway/CVS/GI features — an isolated cutaneous flare, with anaphylaxis safety-netted.
Not present now but must remain safety-netted with a very low escalation threshold.
Rapid urticarial dermatographic picture is not a viral exanthem.

Working telehealth diagnosis: isolated cutaneous mast cell flare; manage with antihistamine up-dosing/cooling, ensure an adrenaline autoinjector/action plan, and escalate to 000 for any systemic feature.

red_flags.description: Any airway/breathing involvement — throat tightness, voice change, wheeze, difficulty breathing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals evolving anaphylaxis requiring adrenaline and 000.

red_flags.description: Circulatory compromise — dizziness/faintness on standing, collapse
GI involvement — vomiting, cramps, diarrhoea with the reaction
red_flags.clinical_significance: A systemic anaphylaxis criterion when combined with skin/other features.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening airway/breathing/circulation/GI, or not safety-netting progression in a patient living alone.
Sending an isolated cutaneous flare to ED by ambulance when antihistamine up-dosing, cooling and safety-netting are appropriate.
Attempting to arrange IM promethazine/other injectables over chat.
Not checking whether an MCAS patient living alone has an adrenaline autoinjector and action plan.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Skin-only involvement without airway/CVS/GI features — does not meet anaphylaxis criteria at present
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports cutaneous management with a very low threshold to escalate if systemic features appear.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure and objective circulatory status
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen anaphylaxis by history, advise antihistamine up-dosing and cooling, ensure/advise an adrenaline autoinjector and ASCIA action plan, and escalate to adrenaline + 000 immediately for any airway/breathing/circulatory/GI feature.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either missing evolving anaphylaxis in an isolated patient, or over-treating a purely cutaneous flare with remote injections/ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Anaphylaxis is defined by airway/breathing/circulation involvement (± persistent severe GI); adrenaline is first-line. Isolated urticaria without these features is managed with antihistamines and observation.
Acute urticaria is treated with non-sedating H1 antihistamines, which can be up-dosed; add H2 antagonists/short steroids in severe cases per guidance.
